section.title: The Good Shepherd
section.sectionRationale: Flowing out of the expulsion of the healed man by false shepherds, Jesus presents Himself as the true shepherd of God's flock. He is both the gate through which the sheep are saved and the good shepherd who lays down His life for them. At the winter Feast of Dedication the theme sharpens into a confrontation over His identity, culminating in the claim, I and the Father are one, and the promise that no one can snatch His sheep from His hand.
section.thesis: Jesus is the door by which the sheep are saved and the good shepherd who willingly lays down His life and takes it up again for His flock, gathering one flock from many folds, so that His sheep hear His voice, follow Him, receive eternal life, and can never be snatched from the hand of the Son who is one with the Father.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the parable of the sheepfold and the true shepherd's voice; I am the gate and I am the good shepherd who lays down His life; the division His words cause; and, at the Feast of Dedication, the security of the sheep and the claim I and the Father are one, met with stones.

verses.text: “Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever does not enter the sheepfold by the gate, but climbs in some other way, is a thief and a robber.
But the one who enters by the gate is the shepherd of the sheep.
The gatekeeper opens the gate for him, and the sheep listen for his voice. He calls his own sheep by name and leads them out.
When he has brought out all his own, he goes on ahead of them, and his sheep follow him because they know his voice.
But they will never follow a stranger; in fact, they will flee from him because they do not recognize his voice.”
Jesus spoke to them using this illustration, but they did not understand what He was telling them.

units.label: I am the gate and the good shepherd
units.note: the shepherd lays down His life

verses.text: So He said to them again, “Truly, truly, I tell you, I am the gate for the sheep.
All who came before Me were thieves and robbers, but the sheep did not listen to them.
{{egoeimi|I am}} the {{thura|gate}}. If anyone enters through Me, he will be saved. He will come in and go out and find pasture.
The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy. I have come that they may have life, and have it in all its fullness.
I am the {{poimen|good shepherd}}. The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep.
The hired hand is not the shepherd, and the sheep are not his own. When he sees the wolf coming, he abandons the sheep and runs away. Then the wolf pounces on them and scatters the flock.
The man runs away because he is a hired servant and is unconcerned for the sheep.
I am the good shepherd. I know My sheep and My sheep know Me,
just as the Father knows Me and I know the Father. And I lay down My life for the sheep.
I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them in as well, and they will listen to My voice. Then there will be one flock and one shepherd.
The reason the Father loves Me is that I lay down My life in order to take it up again.
No one takes it from Me, but I lay it down of My own accord. I have authority to lay it down and authority to take it up again. This charge I have received from My Father.”

units.label: Division over Jesus
units.note: can a demon open the eyes of the blind?

verses.text: Again there was division among the Jews because of Jesus’ message.
Many of them said, “He is demon-possessed and insane. Why would you listen to Him?”
But others replied, “These are not the words of a man possessed by a demon. Can a demon open the eyes of the blind?”

units.label: At the Feast of Dedication
units.note: I and the Father are one

verses.text: At that time the Feast of Dedication took place in Jerusalem. It was winter,
and Jesus was walking in the temple courts in Solomon’s Colonnade.
So the Jews gathered around Him and demanded, “How long will You keep us in suspense? If You are the Christ, tell us plainly.”
“I already told you,” Jesus replied, “but you did not believe. The works I do in My Father’s name testify on My behalf.
But because you are not My sheep, you refuse to believe.
My sheep listen to My voice; I know them, and they follow Me.
I give them {{zoe|eternal life}}, and they will never perish. No one can snatch them out of My hand.
My Father who has given them to Me is greater than all. No one can snatch them out of My Father’s hand.
I and the Father are one.”
At this, the Jews again picked up stones to stone Him.
But Jesus responded, “I have shown you many good works from the Father. For which of these do you stone Me?”
“We are not stoning You for any good work,” said the Jews, “but for blasphemy, because You, who are a man, make Yourself out to be God.”
Jesus replied, “Is it not written in your Law: ‘I have said you are gods’?
If he called them gods to whom the word of God came, and the Scripture cannot be broken,
then what about the One whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world? How then can you accuse Me of blasphemy for stating that I am the Son of God?
If I am not doing the works of My Father, then do not believe Me.
But if I am doing them, even though you do not believe Me, believe the works themselves, so that you may know and understand that the Father is in Me, and I am in the Father.”
At this, they tried again to seize Him, but He escaped their grasp.
Then Jesus went back across the Jordan to the place where John had first been baptizing, and He stayed there.
Many came to Him and said, “Although John never performed a sign, everything he said about this man was true.”
And many in that place believed in Jesus.

egoeimi.gloss: I am. Two more of the great I am sayings appear here, I am the gate and I am the good shepherd. As elsewhere in John, the phrase both completes a rich image and quietly carries the resonance of the divine name, reaching its sharpest point in the claim that Jesus and the Father are one, which His hearers rightly take as a claim to deity.

thura.gloss: Door, gate. Jesus is the gate for the sheep: the only true entrance to salvation, safety, and pasture. To enter through Him is to be saved; all who bypass Him and climb in another way are thieves and robbers. The image insists that access to God and His flock is found in Christ alone.

poimen.gloss: Shepherd. Against the false shepherds of Israel condemned by the prophets, Jesus is the good shepherd who knows His sheep by name and lays down His life for them. The image draws on God as the shepherd of His people and on the promise of a coming Davidic shepherd; Jesus fulfills both, tending, gathering, and dying for the flock.

zoe.gloss: Life, eternal life. Jesus came that the sheep may have life in all its fullness, and He gives them eternal life so that they will never perish. This life is held securely, for no one can snatch the sheep from His hand or the Father's; the gift of life is inseparable from the safety of the flock in the shepherd's keeping.

basic.context: After the false shepherds of Israel have just thrown a healed man out of the synagogue, Jesus describes the true shepherd, whose sheep know his voice and follow him. He then applies it: He is the gate through which the sheep are saved, and the good shepherd who lays down His life for them, unlike the hired hand who flees. He speaks of other sheep to be gathered into one flock. Later, at the winter Feast of Dedication, the leaders press Him to say plainly if He is the Christ. He points to His works, promises His sheep can never be snatched away, and says, I and the Father are one. They take up stones for blasphemy.
basic.hermeneutics: The shepherd imagery is loaded with Old Testament meaning: God is Israel's shepherd, the leaders were false shepherds, and a coming David would shepherd the flock. Jesus claims all of it. The key questions are, who is the true shepherd, and how safe are His sheep. Jesus answers that He alone is the door and the shepherd, that He dies willingly and rises, that His sheep are utterly secure in His hand and the Father's, and that He and the Father are one, a claim to deity His hearers understood exactly.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is built on two I am sayings. Jesus is the thura, the gate, the only entrance to salvation and pasture, and He is the poimen, the good shepherd, who lays down His life for the sheep. He gives them zoe, eternal life, so that they will never perish, held fast by both the Son and the Father, who are ego eimi, one.
basic.typology: Jesus fulfills the picture of God as the shepherd of Israel and the promise of one shepherd, a new David, over the flock. He is the true shepherd against the false shepherds condemned by the prophets, and He gathers the scattered and the other sheep, the nations, into one flock.
basic.christ: Christ is the gate and the good shepherd, who knows His sheep and lays down His life for them of His own accord, with authority to take it up again. He gives eternal life and keeps His sheep unsnatchable. He claims oneness with the Father, an unmistakable claim to deity.
basic.god: God is the shepherd of His people, now present in the Son. The Father gives the sheep to the Son, holds them in His hand, and loves the Son for laying down His life. The Father and the Son are one in keeping the flock secure forever.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 condemns false shepherds and promises God and a Davidic shepherd. Psalm 23 sings of the LORD as shepherd. Isaiah 40 pictures God tending His flock. 1 Peter 5 calls Christ the chief Shepherd.
basic.oneStory: The prophets promised that God Himself would come to shepherd His scattered flock and raise up one shepherd from David's line. In Jesus both come true: the good shepherd lays down His life, gathers one flock from many folds, and keeps His sheep forever, for He and the Father are one.

context.literary: A shepherd discourse arising from chapter 9, developed through two I am sayings and the contrast of true shepherd, hired hand, thief, and wolf. The scene shifts at verse 22 to the Feast of Dedication, where the shepherd theme returns in the security of the sheep and climaxes in I and the Father are one. The section ends with a retreat beyond the Jordan and many believing, a quiet contrast to the hostility in Jerusalem.
context.historical: Palestinian shepherding illumines the imagery: sheep of several flocks were kept in a common fold at night and called out each morning by their own shepherd's voice. The prophets, especially Ezekiel 34, denounced Israel's leaders as false shepherds, which frames Jesus' claim over against them. The Feast of Dedication, Hanukkah, celebrated the rededication of the temple after its defilement, an apt backdrop as Jesus, the one the Father sanctified and sent, speaks in the temple in winter.
context.canonical: The good shepherd fulfills Ezekiel 34, where God promises to shepherd His flock Himself and to set one shepherd, His servant David, over them, and Psalm 23 and Zechariah's shepherd oracles. The gathering of other sheep into one flock anticipates the inclusion of the Gentiles, John 11:52 and Ephesians 2. The claim I and the Father are one stands among the New Testament's strongest statements of the Son's unity with the Father, and the security of the sheep grounds the doctrine of the believer's preservation, Romans 8:38-39.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus as the only door of salvation and the good shepherd who dies and rises for His flock, to assure His sheep of their eternal security in His hand and the Father's, and to press the claim of His oneness with the Father, so that readers will hear His voice and follow Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The discourse describes the relationship of the shepherd and his sheep in image, but it teaches abiding realities: Christ alone is the way of salvation, His people are eternally secure, and true sheep hear and follow His voice. The call to enter by the gate and follow the shepherd is for every reader.

debates.question: What does I and the Father are one mean?

views.label: Unity of nature and deity
views.case: The hearers immediately charge blasphemy because a man makes Himself God, and the surrounding claims about giving eternal life and holding the sheep in His hand support a oneness of divine nature and power, not merely of purpose.

views.label: Unity of will and action in keeping the sheep
views.case: The immediate context is that no one can snatch the sheep from the Son's hand or the Father's, so the oneness is at least a perfect unity of action in saving and keeping the flock.

debates.bearing: The two are not opposed: the Son and the Father act as one in salvation precisely because they are one in nature, which is why Jesus goes on to speak of the Father in Him and Himself in the Father.

debates.question: How does Jesus use Psalm 82, I have said you are gods, in verses 34 to 36?

views.label: A lesser-to-greater argument
views.case: If Scripture could call human judges, to whom God's word came, gods, then it is no blasphemy for the one whom the Father uniquely sanctified and sent to call Himself the Son of God; He argues from the lesser to the greater.

views.label: An appeal to Scripture's authority against the charge
views.case: Jesus stresses that the Scripture cannot be broken, using the leaders' own Law to silence the blasphemy charge and turn attention to His works as evidence.

debates.bearing: Either way Jesus is not lowering His claim but disarming the accusation, then pointing to His works so they may know the Father is in Him and He in the Father, a claim far higher than the judges of Psalm 82.

study.originalLanguages: The discourse rests on two I am sayings. Jesus is the thura, the gate for the sheep, the sole entrance through which anyone is saved and finds pasture, so that all who bypass Him are thieves and robbers. And He is the poimen, the good shepherd, who unlike the hireling knows His sheep by name and lays down His life for them, fulfilling the prophets' promise of a true shepherd over God's flock. He gives His sheep zoe, eternal life, so that they will never perish and cannot be snatched from His hand or the Father's. And He gathers these claims into the words I and the Father are one, ego eimi language that His hearers rightly hear as a claim to share the being of God.

typology.type: God as the shepherd of Israel, Psalm 23 and Ezekiel 34
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the good shepherd, who tends, gathers, and lays down His life for the flock, John 10:11.
Christ, the one shepherd over one flock gathered from many folds, John 10:16.
The One whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world, the true holy presence of God, John 10:36.

study.christ: Christ presents Himself as both the door and the shepherd. As the gate, He is the one entrance to salvation, safety, and pasture; no one comes to the Father's flock except through Him. As the good shepherd, He knows His sheep intimately and lays down His life for them, not as a helpless victim but of His own accord, with authority to lay it down and to take it up again. He gathers a single flock from every fold, gives His sheep eternal life, and holds them so securely that none can be snatched away. And He declares His oneness with the Father, a claim to deity that His hearers grasp at once when they reach for stones.
study.god: God is the shepherd of His people, the one who in the prophets promised to come and tend His scattered flock Himself, now present in the Son. The Father gives the sheep to the Son, holds them in His own hand, and loves the Son for laying down His life. Father and Son act as one in saving and keeping the flock, because they are one. The God who sanctified the temple has now sanctified and sent His Son into the world, and the security of every believer rests on the united, unbreakable grip of the Father and the Son.

crossReferences.note: God promises to search for His sheep and shepherd them Himself, condemning the false shepherds, fulfilled in Jesus.
I will set over them one shepherd, My servant David, the promise of the Davidic shepherd Jesus fulfills.
The LORD is my shepherd, the confidence of the flock now embodied in Christ the good shepherd.
Nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ, echoing that no one can snatch the sheep from His hand.

study.oneStory: The prophets promised that God Himself would come to shepherd His scattered, mistreated flock, and that He would raise up one shepherd from David's line to tend them. In Jesus both promises come true at once. The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep and takes it up again, gathers one flock from many folds, and holds His people so securely that none can ever be lost, for He and the Father are one. Against the backdrop of false shepherds who scatter and devour, the true shepherd has come to give His life and to keep His flock forever.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by saying I am the gate for the sheep?

options.text: He is the only true entrance to salvation, safety, and pasture; whoever enters through Him is saved.
options.note: Verse 9 ties entering through Him to being saved and finding pasture.

options.text: He is one of several ways people may come to God.
options.note: The image insists He is the gate; all who bypass Him are thieves and robbers.

options.text: He guards the sheep by standing at the entrance.
options.note: He does protect the flock, but the claim is that He Himself is the only door of salvation.

options.text: He is the way into God's flock and its safe pasture.
options.note: True, but it softens the exclusive claim that anyone saved must enter through Him alone.

quiz.q: What distinguishes the good shepherd from the hired hand?

options.text: The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep, while the hired hand flees when the wolf comes.
options.note: Verses 11 to 13 contrast the shepherd's self-sacrifice with the hireling's flight.

options.text: The good shepherd owns more sheep than the hired hand.
options.note: The difference is love and sacrifice, not the size of the flock.

options.text: The good shepherd knows the sheep and cares for them.
options.note: True, but it omits the decisive mark, that He lays down His life for them.

options.text: The good shepherd is paid more for his work.
options.note: The point is the opposite of hire; He gives His life freely, not for wages.

quiz.q: How secure are Jesus' sheep, according to verses 28 and 29?

options.text: They have eternal life, will never perish, and no one can snatch them from His hand or the Father's.
options.note: Verses 28 and 29 promise complete security in the hands of the Son and the Father.

options.text: They are safe only as long as they hold on tightly enough.
options.note: Their security rests on the shepherd's grip, not their own strength.

options.text: They are kept safe in the Father's hand.
options.note: True, but it leaves out that they are equally held in the Son's hand and given eternal life that never perishes.

options.text: They are protected from most dangers but not all.
options.note: The promise is absolute: no one can snatch them away, not a partial safety.

quiz.q: Why did the crowd pick up stones after Jesus said I and the Father are one?

options.text: Because they understood Him to be claiming to be God, which they called blasphemy.
options.note: Verse 33 states they stoned Him for making Himself out to be God.

options.text: Because He had broken the Sabbath again.
options.note: Sabbath was an earlier charge; here the explicit issue is His claim to be one with the Father.

options.text: Because He refused to answer whether He was the Christ.
options.note: He did answer, pointing to His works and His oneness with the Father.

options.text: Because He claimed a unique unity with the Father.
options.note: True, but it does not name that they specifically charged Him with blasphemy for making Himself God.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say about the other sheep not of this fold?

options.text: He must bring them in too, so there will be one flock and one shepherd.
options.note: Verse 16 anticipates the gathering of others into a single flock under Him.

options.text: They will always remain separate from His flock.
options.note: The opposite; He will bring them in to form one flock.

options.text: They are other Jewish believers not yet gathered in.
options.note: The other sheep most likely include the Gentiles, not only scattered Jews; either way Jesus gathers them into one flock.

options.text: They will also hear His voice and follow Him.
options.note: True, but it omits the goal, one flock and one shepherd.

questions: Jesus says His sheep know His voice. How well do you recognize His voice amid the many voices competing for your attention?
The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep of His own accord. How does it move you that Christ's death was a free gift of love, not a defeat forced on Him?
No one can snatch Jesus' sheep from His hand. Where do you need the comfort of that security today?
Jesus came to give life in all its fullness, against the thief who steals and destroys. Where are you settling for a thin, robbed life instead of the fullness He offers?
Jesus gathers other sheep into one flock. How does His heart for those still outside shape the way you pray for and reach people far from Him?

translationNotes.note: The Feast of Dedication; the BSB footnote explains this is Hanukkah, celebrating the Maccabean rededication of the temple. It is a fitting backdrop as Jesus speaks of being sanctified and sent by the Father.
I have said you are gods quotes Psalm 82:6, addressed to human judges; Jesus argues from the lesser to the greater to answer the charge of blasphemy.
The Scripture cannot be broken affirms the unbreakable authority of Scripture, on which Jesus rests His argument.
One flock and one shepherd; the other sheep not of this fold anticipate the gathering of the Gentiles into one people under Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 10:1-42. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Em dashes framing the clause in verse 35 (and the Scripture cannot be broken) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
